expectations.text: Explains that shadow size/softness communicates elevation — closer elements have larger, softer shadows
expectations.evidence: 'Objects close to a surface cast short, tight, dark shadows. Objects far from a surface cast long, wide, diffuse shadows.' — clear physics analogy explaining the relationship

expectations.text: Justifies why the modal should have a larger/heavier shadow than the tooltip based on elevation
expectations.evidence: 'Elevation Hierarchy: Modals represent high elevation...Tooltips are low elevation — a small label that barely lifts off the surface. Matching shadow values collapses this distinction'

expectations.text: Includes specific CSS box-shadow values for at least one element
expectations.evidence: Modal: `0 20px 60px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.30), 0 8px 20px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15)`; Tooltip: `0 4px 12px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15)` — both provided

expectations.text: Recommends building a reusable shadow scale/system rather than ad-hoc values
expectations.evidence: 'Use the same shadow system — not the same shadow value. Define a shadow scale (like shadow-sm, shadow-md, shadow-xl) and assign modals to the highest tier and tooltips to a lower tier'

suggestions.reason: The shadow eval is not discriminating — general knowledge handles it well. Consider adding an assertion specifically about two-part shadows (ambient + direct) which is Refactoring UI-specific advice the skill provides but generic knowledge does not

eval_feedback.overall: Both with_skill and without_skill pass all assertions. This eval needs stronger Refactoring UI-specific assertions to be discriminating.
